seo.title: AG2 Guardrails for Cybersecurity
seo.description: Compliance guide for AG2 agents in cybersecurity. Meet NIST Cybersecurity Framework, SOC 2 Type II requirements with Vex guardrails.

content.intro: Cybersecurity teams using AG2 for multi-agent threat intelligence analysis and incident response face heightened risks due to the framework's conversational dynamics, which can spiral into off-topic discussions or biased group consensus on critical facts. Without guardrails, agents may leak sensitive threat data via LLM APIs or trigger unapproved automated responses, violating NIST CSF and ISO 27001. Vex provides essential compliance hooks to monitor and enforce regulations in these high-stakes environments.

complianceRequirements.description: Requires identification of sensitive threat intelligence and protection against unauthorized disclosure in AI-driven workflows.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex message hooks scan AG2 conversations for PII/threat data leakage, blocking prompts containing classified intel before LLM transmission.

complianceRequirements.description: Mandates controls for AI-generated outputs to prevent false positives in incident detection and response.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex validates agent consensus against CVE databases and flags unverified security advisories in group chats.

complianceRequirements.regulation: ISO 27001 - A.12.4.1 Event Logging & A.14.2.7 Secure Development
complianceRequirements.description: Requires audit trails for multi-agent decisions and human approval gates for automated security actions.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex logs speaker selection and conversation drift, enforcing human-in-loop for critical response actions.

complianceRequirements.description: Prohibits unverified AI outputs in critical infrastructure and mandates verification against authoritative sources.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex integrates CVE/NVD API checks on code execution agents to prevent harmful or incorrect remediation scripts.

industryRisks.name: Threat Intelligence Leakage
industryRisks.description: AG2 group chats expose IOCs/TTPs to LLM providers during threat analysis conversations.
industryRisks.example: Adversary queries LLM with scraped AG2 conversation containing zero-day indicators.

industryRisks.name: False Positive Incident Escalation
industryRisks.description: Multi-agent consensus on incorrect threat facts triggers automated shutdowns.
industryRisks.example: Code agent generates firewall rules blocking legitimate traffic based on hallucinated CVE.

industryRisks.name: Speaker Selection Bias
industryRisks.description: Biased speaker rotation in group chat leads to echo chamber on threat prioritization.
industryRisks.example: Junior analyst agent dominates, downplaying critical ransomware indicators.

industryRisks.name: Unapproved Automated Response
industryRisks.description: Code execution agents deploy remediation without human oversight.
industryRisks.example: AG2 executes network isolation script causing outage during false positive alert.

implementation.title: Register Vex Threat Data Guardrail
implementation.description: Install Vex SDK and register as AG2 message hook to block threat intel leakage before LLM calls.
implementation.code:
import vex
import ag2

# Install: pip install ag2 vex-sdk

@vex.hook(guards=["pii", "threat_intel"])
def cybersecurity_message_hook(message, agent, groupchat):
    if 'ioc' in message.lower() or 'ttp' in message.lower():
        raise vex.Block("Threat intelligence detected - human review required")
    return message

# Register with AG2 group chat
config_list = ag2.config_list_llm
manager = ag2.GroupChatManager(
    agents=[analyst, responder],
    message_hooks=[cybersecurity_message_hook]
)

implementation.title: CVE Verification for Code Agents
implementation.description: Validate security advisories against NVD before code execution in AG2 workflows.

implementation.title: Human Approval for Critical Actions
implementation.description: Enforce human-in-loop for automated response actions in AG2 group chats.

caseStudyScenario.heading: AG2 in Cybersecurity: A Scenario
caseStudyScenario.scenario:
A SOC team at a financial institution deploys AG2 for real-time threat hunting across their perimeter defenses. The multi-agent group consists of a TriageAgent that parses SIEM alerts, ThreatAnalystAgent that correlates IOCs against threat intel feeds, and CodeResponderAgent that generates firewall rules and EDR queries. During a phishing campaign investigation, the group chat identifies a novel TTP matching a fresh ransomware strain. The conversation spirals as ThreatAnalystAgent shares unredacted IOCs (IPs, hashes) while debating attribution, and CodeResponderAgent prepares network isolation scripts based on group consensus.

Without guardrails, the sensitive IOCs get embedded in LLM prompts sent to external APIs, and the unverified consensus triggers automatic firewall updates blocking 15% of customer VPN traffic. Adversaries monitoring LLM data lakes later exploit the leaked indicators for their C2 infrastructure.
caseStudyScenario.outcome: With Vex message hooks registered on the AG2 group chat, the cybersecurity_message_hook immediately flagged and blocked IOC leakage before LLM transmission, notifying the SOC manager via Slack. The verify_cve_advisory hook paused code execution when consensus referenced an unverified CVE, forcing human validation against internal threat feeds. Human approval gates prevented premature network isolation, allowing the team to confirm the threat scope. The incident was contained without service disruption, maintaining SOC 2 compliance while preserving all audit trails for ISO 27001 certification.

cta.heading: Comply with Cybersecurity Regulations Using Vex
cta.description: Register Vex on your AG2 deployments today to prevent threat leakage and enforce human oversight on critical actions.
